stories.headline: Brothers sentenced to decades in prison for 2024 drive-by shooting outside Denver Downtown Aquarium
stories.summary:
Antonio Vasquez and Jason Trujillo Jr. were sentenced Friday for their roles in the 2024 drive-by shooting death of 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Downtown Aquarium in Denver. Vasquez received 40 years in prison and Trujillo received 20 years following their convictions.

Denver District Attorney John Walsh called the shooting — which occurred in broad daylight in a busy public location — a crime that 'shocked countless Denver residents.' Walsh said the sentences ensure the brothers 'will pay a heavy price for their cold-blooded actions.'

The case drew significant public attention given its location at one of Denver's prominent family attractions. The sentencing marks the conclusion of a prosecution that began after Salazar was killed in the drive-by attack in 2024.

stories.headline: Three arrested on suspicion of murder in Denver slaying near Cherry Creek Reservoir
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested three men this week on suspicion of second-degree murder in connection with the death of 47-year-old Neal Jones Jr., who was killed near Cherry Creek Reservoir in early February. Officers responded to an assault just after 2 a.m. Sunday in the 9700 block of East Hampden Avenue, near the Kennedy Soccer Complex, and Jones was pronounced dead at HCA HealthONE Swedish hospital.

Investigators arrested Derek Wafford, 39; Demetrius Davis, 37; and Gregory Manard, 31, on Thursday. All three are being held at the Downtown Detention Center, with Wafford and Manard each on $100,000 bail.

Detectives identified the suspects through an investigation that included a Crime Stoppers tip, a license plate reader hit and video evidence, according to Denver police. Manard is due in court March 9. The cause and manner of Jones's death remain pending from the medical examiner.

stories.headline: Thornton man sentenced to nearly 70 years for kidnapping and sexually assaulting teenager
stories.summary:
A Thornton man was sentenced Friday to 68 years to life in prison for kidnapping and sexually assaulting a teenage girl in 2023. Phillip Torres, 44, received the maximum allowable sentence under state law.

The case was prosecuted in the Denver metro area and represents one of the more severe sentences handed down in the region in recent months. Details of the crime and trial were not immediately available beyond the sentencing announcement.

The sentence underscores Colorado courts' application of maximum penalties in cases involving the kidnapping and sexual assault of minors.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance 'second-look' sentencing bill that could free long-serving prisoners
stories.summary:
Democratic state senators Julie Gonzales of Denver and Mike Weissman of Aurora have introduced Senate Bill 26-115, which would create a new legal process allowing judges to reduce sentences for prisoners who have served at least 20 years and who either committed their crimes before age 21 or are now at least 60 years old. An estimated 137 prisoners would automatically qualify to petition for a sentence reduction under the bill.

Prisoners seeking relief would file a petition and face an open-court hearing where they must demonstrate they no longer pose a danger to society. Judges could reduce sentences to no lower than 25 years, potentially enabling immediate parole release for those who have already served two decades. Crime victims would be entitled to speak at hearings.

First-degree murder convictions are excluded from automatic eligibility, though prosecutors could bring petitions for those 164 prisoners at their own discretion. About half of U.S. states have some form of second-look sentencing already in place. Supporters argue the bill addresses limitations in Colorado's existing clemency and post-conviction relief systems.

stories.headline: Out-of-state group proposes Colorado redistricting plan that would yield seven Democratic U.S. House seats
stories.summary:
A political group called Coloradans for a Level Playing Field, backed by out-of-state Democrats, has proposed a redistricting plan that would redraw Colorado's eight congressional districts to favor seven Democratic seats and one Republican. The group is pursuing a ballot measure for the November election; if approved, the new maps would take effect for the 2028 and 2030 House elections.

The plan would divide El Paso County — home to Colorado Springs and currently anchored in the Republican-held 5th Congressional District — into two portions folded into separate new districts. Critics note the proposal mirrors Republican mid-decade redistricting efforts already underway in Texas and other states.

Colorado Democrats have framed the effort as a response to Republican gerrymandering nationally, while opponents argue the plan itself constitutes a gerrymander that concentrates Republican voters into a single Eastern Plains district. The proposal would affect multiple Front Range communities, including portions of Douglas and Arapahoe counties.

stories.headline: Denver International Airport's most popular flight destinations revealed in new analysis
stories.summary:
A new Denver Business Journal analysis of flight data from Denver International Airport shows where millions of passengers travel most frequently, offering insight into the airport's role as a major national hub. The report examines destination trends across DIA's extensive domestic and international route network.

DIA consistently ranks among the busiest airports in the United States, and destination data can inform decisions by airlines, businesses and travelers about route demand and travel patterns through the Rocky Mountain region.

The full analysis, published Saturday by the Denver Business Journal, provides a breakdown of top routes and passenger volume figures for the Denver metro area's primary aviation gateway.

stories.headline: Broncos retain offensive lineman Ben Powers, set to field same starting unit for third straight year
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos will likely field the same starting offensive line — Garett Bolles, Ben Powers, Luke Wattenberg, Quinn Meinerz and Mike McGlinchey — for a third consecutive season after the team informed Powers they have no plans to trade or restructure his contract, sources told The Denver Post. The decision preserves continuity around third-year quarterback Bo Nix, a priority the organization has emphasized throughout his development.

Earlier this week, a source indicated the Broncos were shopping the 29-year-old Powers on the trade market given his $18.2 million cap hit in 2026, but Denver reversed course by Thursday night. Versatile reserve Alex Palczewski, re-signed to a two-year deal, could still push Powers for his starting left-guard spot after starting 10 games in Powers's place last season.

The Broncos acknowledged they will need to address offensive-line depth and long-term youth infusion in coming offseasons, particularly as McGlinchey and Bolles age. Cutting or trading both veterans could free more than $37 million in cap space but would disrupt the continuity the organization has prioritized.

stories.headline: Broncos lock up running back Tyler Badie, tender three other exclusive rights free agents
stories.summary:
Running back Tyler Badie has signed a one-year, $1.075 million contract to return to the Denver Broncos for a fifth season after accepting an exclusive rights free agent tender, a source told the Denver Gazette. Badie appeared in 16 games in 2025, his first full season on the active roster, recording 23 rushing yards and 141 receiving yards.

The Broncos also tendered safety Devon Key, outside linebacker Dondrea Tillman and defensive lineman Jordan Jackson with the same one-year, $1.075 million deals. Exclusive rights free agents who receive tenders cannot sign with another team unless released by their current club.

A key remaining question is the future of tight end Nate Adkins, whom head coach Sean Payton values highly. Placing a second-round tender on Adkins worth $3.52 million could be deemed too costly, potentially prompting a separate negotiated deal. Defensive lineman John Franklin-Myers is expected to depart as an unrestricted free agent when the negotiating window opens Monday.

stories.headline: No. 7 DU hockey sweeps Miami (Ohio), advances to NCHC semifinals against Western Michigan
stories.summary:
The No. 7 University of Denver Pioneers swept Miami (Ohio) in the best-of-three NCHC quarterfinal series, earning a home semifinal matchup against No. 3 seed Western Michigan next Saturday at Magness Arena. Denver won the series finale 6-2 on Saturday night, with six different players scoring and Eric Pohlkamp, Jake Fisher and Kyle Chyzowski each recording a goal and an assist.

The matchup with Western Michigan sets up a rematch of last year's Frozen Four in St. Louis, which the Broncos won in double overtime before advancing to the national championship game. DU and WMU also met in last year's NCHC Frozen Faceoff championship, with Western Michigan winning in double overtime.

Denver has now reached the NCHC semifinals in every season since the conference was founded in 2012-13, a streak unmatched in conference history. The Pioneers have won 22 of their last 23 meetings against the Miami RedHawks.

stories.headline: Avalanche beat Stars in shootout night of Kadri trade, emerge as Central Division's top contenders
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche capped a remarkable trade deadline day by defeating the Dallas Stars 5-4 in a shootout Friday night in Dallas, hours after acquiring center Nazem Kadri from the Calgary Flames. Valeri Nichushkin scored to tie the game with less than a minute remaining in regulation before the Avalanche prevailed in the shootout.

Beyond the on-ice result, Colorado's front office is being credited as the biggest winner of the deadline period. In addition to Kadri — a player the Stars were rumored to be pursuing — the Minnesota Wild failed to acquire top centers Vincent Trocheck or Ryan O'Reilly, leaving two key Central Division rivals without the upgrades they sought.

General manager Chris MacFarland's moves, which also included acquiring center Nicolas Roy from Toronto earlier in the week and defenseman Nick Blankenburg from Nashville, have positioned the Avalanche as a strong Stanley Cup contender heading into the playoff stretch. Analysts note the trades came at a cost to Colorado's prospect depth and future assets.

stories.headline: Colorado College hockey season ends in overtime loss to Western Michigan in NCHC quarterfinals
stories.summary:
Colorado College's hockey season came to a close Saturday night with a 2-1 overtime loss to No. 4 seed Western Michigan in the NCHC quarterfinal series, as WMU swept the Tigers in the best-of-three matchup. The deciding goal came 17 seconds into a power play in overtime, following a controversial slew-footing major and game misconduct called against CC freshman Mateo Mrsic after a lengthy video review.

It was the 13th time this season CC took an opponent to overtime but could not convert. Junior Klavs Veinbergs scored a shorthanded goal for the Tigers' only tally of the night, and freshman goaltender Jackson Unger made 32 saves in his first postseason start, filling in for injured captain Kaidan Mbereko.

Mbereko, who set a program record Friday with his 128th career appearance as CC's goaltender, could not play in the series finale due to injury. The Tigers finished the season 13-17-6. Head coach Kris Mayotte praised his team's fight throughout the year, noting the squad never got on a sustained winning run.

stories.headline: Wings Over the Rockies hosts Women of Aviation event to recruit future female pilots
stories.summary:
Wings Over the Rockies Air and Space Museum partnered with the Colorado chapter of the Ninety-Nines on Saturday to celebrate Women of Aviation Worldwide Week at its Exploration of Flight location at Centennial Airport. The event featured booths, interactive activities and presentations from current and retired female pilots aimed at introducing attendees to aviation careers and scholarships.

The Ninety-Nines, founded in 1929 with Amelia Earhart as its first president, offer scholarships for flight training, aviation college degrees and professional certifications, along with a three-phase mentorship program. Federal Aviation Administration data show that fewer than 11% of all pilots and fewer than 16% of aviation students were women in 2024.

Speakers included retired American Airlines pilot Donna Miller and Heather Bulk, co-founder and CEO of a Colorado aviation company. Organizers said the goal was to expand awareness of aviation pathways for women and girls in the Denver metro area and beyond.

weather.notable: About 14 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Men want the same thing from their underwear that they want from women: a little bit of support, and a little bit of freedom.

topStory.headline: Israel Strikes Iranian Oil Facilities in Tehran, Killing at Least Four
topStory.summary:
Israeli air strikes hit oil storage and production facilities in and around Tehran on Saturday, igniting large fires and killing at least four tanker drivers. The attacked sites included the Aghdasieh oil warehouse in northeast Tehran, the Tehran refinery in the south, the Shahran oil depot in the west, and an oil depot in Karaj city in Alborz province. Witnesses reported oil from the Shahran depot had leaked into surrounding streets, and thick black smoke blanketed large portions of the Iranian capital.

Israel said the targeted fuel storage facilities were being used to support Iranian military infrastructure. Iranian state media characterized the strikes as a joint attack by the United States and Israel, a description that reflects Tehran's position on the broader conflict. Iranian authorities said fuel distribution had not been interrupted and that security forces were engaged in firefighting operations at the affected sites.

The strikes mark the first time Israeli forces have targeted Iranian oil facilities since the current conflict began, according to Al Jazeera's correspondent in Tehran. The reporter described witnessing black residue falling on windows early Sunday morning and warned of serious risks from toxic air in the capital. He noted the attacks on civil industrial infrastructure represented an escalation beyond previous strikes on fuel depots seen during an earlier round of fighting in June.

The strikes on Saturday came on the ninth consecutive day of joint Israeli and U.S. operations against Iran, which have killed more than 1,300 people in Iran and approximately 300 in Lebanon, according to the report. About a dozen people have been killed in Israel. Analysts cited in the reporting suggested the oil facility strikes serve both a strategic purpose — limiting Iranian troop mobility — and a psychological one, though Iran's size and the breadth of its fuel infrastructure make a total supply crisis unlikely in the near term.
